title: Geometric vectors & the dot/cross product
summary: Geometric vectors in the plane and space: components $\vec v=v_x\vec i+v_y\vec j+v_z\vec k$, sum, scalar multiple, and norm. The dot product computes angles and tests orthogonality ($\vec u\cdot\vec w=0$); the cross product tests parallelism ($\vec u\times\vec w=\vec 0$) and gives areas; the mixed product gives volumes and tests coplanarity ($\vec u\times\vec v\cdot\vec w=0$). These products are exactly the linear-(in)dependence detectors for two and three geometric vectors, and drive triangle classification, collinearity, projection, and parametric exam problems."
objectives: Write 2D/3D geometric vectors in components and compute sums, scalar multiples, and norms
Use the dot product to find angles, test orthogonality, and project one vector onto another
Use the cross product to test parallelism and compute parallelogram and triangle areas
Use the mixed (triple) product to compute box volumes and test coplanarity
Translate parallel/coplanar conditions into linear (in)dependence of geometric vectors
Classify triangles and test collinearity of points from coordinates
Solve parametric coplanarity and cross-product equations as on the written exam

blocks.text: A **geometric (free) vector** $\vec v$ carries three pieces of information: a **direction** (the line it lies on), a **verse** (which way along that line), and a **magnitude** (length) $\lVert\vec v\rVert$. An **applied vector** is a free vector plus an application point. From two points $A$ and $B$ we build the applied vector $\overrightarrow{AB} = B - A$; the reverse $\overrightarrow{BA}$ has the same direction and length but opposite verse. The whole topic reduces geometry (lengths, angles, areas, volumes) to simple component arithmetic.

Fixing orthogonal axes gives three unit vectors $\vec i,\vec j,\vec k$. Every free vector in space writes uniquely as $$\vec v = v_x\,\vec i + v_y\,\vec j + v_z\,\vec k,$$ and the numbers $v_x,v_y,v_z$ are its **components**. In the plane there is simply no $z$-component. If $\vec v$ is applied at the origin, its head sits at the point $(v_x,v_y,v_z)$.

blocks.tex: a\vec v + b\vec w = (av_x+bw_x)\vec i + (av_y+bw_y)\vec j + (av_z+bw_z)\vec k
blocks.caption: Sum and scalar multiple act component by component. Scalar $c$ scales length by $|c|$ and flips verse when $c<0$; the sum follows the parallelogram (or tip-to-tail) rule.

blocks.text: The **norm** is $\lVert\vec v\rVert = \sqrt{v_x^2+v_y^2+v_z^2}$, recovered from the dot product as $\lVert\vec v\rVert=\sqrt{\vec v\cdot\vec v}$. A vector of length one is a **versor** (unit vector); for any $\vec v\neq\vec 0$, $\dfrac{\vec v}{\lVert\vec v\rVert}$ is the versor with the same direction and verse.

For vectors $\vec u,\vec w$ with angle $\alpha$ between them ($0\le\alpha\le\pi$): $$\vec u\cdot\vec w = \lVert\vec u\rVert\,\lVert\vec w\rVert\cos\alpha.$$ The result is a **scalar** (a number), not a vector. It is symmetric, linear in each argument, and positive: $\vec u\cdot\vec u=\lVert\vec u\rVert^2\ge 0$.

blocks.tex: \vec v\cdot\vec w = v_xw_x + v_yw_y + v_zw_z, \qquad \cos\alpha = \frac{\vec u\cdot\vec w}{\lVert\vec u\rVert\,\lVert\vec w\rVert}
blocks.caption: Component formula for the dot product, and the angle it computes. The orthogonal projection of $\vec u$ onto $\vec w$ is the vector $\dfrac{\vec u\cdot\vec w}{\lVert\vec w\rVert^2}\,\vec w$.

blocks.text: **Orthogonality test.** For non-zero $\vec u,\vec w$: $\;\vec u\perp\vec w \iff \vec u\cdot\vec w = 0$. This single equation is the workhorse for checking right angles, rectangles, and perpendicularity throughout the course.

The dot product returns a **number**, the cross product returns a **vector**. Writing $\vec u\cdot\vec w = \vec 0$ (a vector) or $\vec u\times\vec w = 0$ (a scalar) is a type error that costs marks. Also $\vec u\cdot\vec w=0$ means *orthogonal*, while $\vec u\times\vec w=\vec 0$ means *parallel* — do not swap them.

$\vec u\times\vec w$ is the unique vector that is (i) **orthogonal** to both $\vec u$ and $\vec w$, (ii) has magnitude $\lVert\vec u\times\vec w\rVert = \lVert\vec u\rVert\,\lVert\vec w\rVert\sin\alpha$, and (iii) has verse given by the **right-hand rule** (fingers sweep $\vec u$ onto $\vec w$, thumb points along $\vec u\times\vec w$). It is **skew-symmetric**: $\vec u\times\vec w = -\,\vec w\times\vec u$, and $\vec u\times\vec u = \vec 0$.

blocks.tex: \vec v\times\vec w = (v_yw_z - v_zw_y)\,\vec i - (v_xw_z - v_zw_x)\,\vec j + (v_xw_y - v_yw_x)\,\vec k
blocks.caption: Component formula. Note the MINUS sign on the $\vec j$ component — this is the most common arithmetic slip. The basis relations are $\vec i\times\vec j=\vec k,\ \vec j\times\vec k=\vec i,\ \vec k\times\vec i=\vec j$ (cyclic).

blocks.text: **Parallelism + area.** For non-zero $\vec u,\vec w$: $\;\vec u\parallel\vec w \iff \vec u\times\vec w=\vec 0$. And $\lVert\vec u\times\vec w\rVert = \text{area of the parallelogram}$ on $\vec u,\vec w = 2\cdot\text{area of the triangle}$. So three points $A,B,C$ are collinear $\iff \overrightarrow{AB}\times\overrightarrow{AC}=\vec 0$.

For three vectors, $\;\vec u\times\vec v\cdot\vec w\;$ (no parentheses needed — the cross must come first). Its absolute value $|\vec u\times\vec v\cdot\vec w|$ is the **volume of the box** (parallelepiped) on $\vec u,\vec v,\vec w$. Swapping two vectors flips the sign; cyclic shifts leave it unchanged.

**Coplanarity test.** For non-zero $\vec u,\vec v,\vec w$: $\;\vec u\times\vec v\cdot\vec w = 0 \iff$ the three vectors are **coplanar** (lie in one plane), equivalently the box is flat / they are **linearly dependent**. This is the standard way the exam asks you to find a parameter $t$.

**Linear (in)dependence**, the geometric reading: two vectors are dependent $\iff$ parallel $\iff$ $\vec u\times\vec w=\vec 0$. Three vectors are dependent $\iff$ coplanar $\iff$ $\vec u\times\vec v\cdot\vec w=0$. So the cross and mixed products are exactly the dependence detectors for 2 and 3 geometric vectors.

steps.label: Side lengths
steps.text: Compute each norm $\lVert\,\cdot\,\rVert=\sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2}$. Equal lengths $\Rightarrow$ equilateral/isosceles.

steps.label: Right angle?
steps.text: Dot two edges sharing a vertex; if a dot product is $0$ the triangle is right-angled there.

steps.label: Area
steps.text: $\text{Area}=\tfrac12\lVert\overrightarrow{AB}\times\overrightarrow{AC}\rVert$. If this is $0$ the points are collinear (degenerate triangle).

blocks.title: Worked: triangle A(1,0,0), B(0,1,0), C(0,0,1)
blocks.text: Edges: $\overrightarrow{AB}=B-A=(-1,1,0)=-\vec i+\vec j$, $\overrightarrow{BC}=C-B=(0,-1,1)=-\vec j+\vec k$, $\overrightarrow{CA}=A-C=(1,0,-1)=\vec i-\vec k$. Each length is $\sqrt{1+1}=\sqrt2$, so $T$ is **equilateral** and the perimeter is $3\sqrt2$. For area use $\overrightarrow{AB}\times\overrightarrow{AC}$ with $\overrightarrow{AC}=C-A=(-1,0,1)$: the cross product is $(-1,1,0)\times(-1,0,1)=(1\cdot1-0\cdot0,\;-((-1)\cdot1-0\cdot(-1)),\;(-1)\cdot0-1\cdot(-1))=(1,1,1)$, length $\sqrt3$. So $\text{Area}=\tfrac12\sqrt3=\tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}$.

question.prompt: Which equation correctly tests whether non-zero $\vec u$ and $\vec w$ are parallel?
question.explanation: Parallel means the cross product vanishes, $\vec u\times\vec w=\vec 0$ (zero area, $\sin\alpha=0$). A tests orthogonality, not parallelism. C is meaningless as a parallel test. D (equal lengths) says nothing about direction.

question.prompt: Vectors $\vec u,\vec v,\vec w$ satisfy $\vec u\times\vec v\cdot\vec w = 0$ with all three non-zero. What can you conclude?

options.content: They span a box of volume 0, i.e. they are coplanar / linearly dependent
Their sum is the zero vector

question.explanation: A zero mixed product means zero box volume, so the three vectors lie in a common plane — they are coplanar, equivalently linearly dependent. Orthogonality (A) would instead make the volume maximal. Parallel (C) is a stronger, unneeded condition. The sum (D) is unrelated.
